verses.text: At that time, Jesus walked through grainfields on the Sabbath. His disciples were hungry, so they plucked the heads of grain and ate.
When the Pharisees saw this, they objected: “Look, your disciples are doing what is not lawful on the Sabbath!”
He said to them: “Have you not read what David did when he was hungry, and those with him—
how he entered the house of God and ate the bread of the Presence, which only priests were permitted to eat?
Or have you not read in the Law that the priests in the temple break the Sabbath and are guiltless?
But I tell you, something greater than the temple is here.
If you had known the meaning of ‘I desire mercy, not sacrifice,’ you would not have condemned the guiltless.
For the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath.”
Then Jesus went on from there and entered their synagogue.
A man with a withered hand was there. They asked him, “Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?” with the intent to accuse him.
He replied, “Which of you, if you had a sheep that fell into a pit on the Sabbath, would not take hold of it and lift it out?
How much more valuable is a person than a sheep! Therefore, it is lawful to do good on the Sabbath.”
Then he said to the man, “Stretch out your hand.” He stretched it out, and it was fully restored like the other.
But the Pharisees went out and plotted against him to destroy him.
Jesus, aware of this, withdrew from there. Many followed him, and he healed them all.
And he warned them not to make him known,
to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah:
“Behold, My Servant whom I have chosen; My Beloved in whom My soul is well-pleased.
He will not quarrel or cry out, nor will His voice be heard in the streets.
A battered reed He will not break off, a smoldering wick He will not put out, until He leads justice to victory.
And in His name the Gentiles will hope.”
They brought Jesus a demon-possessed man who was blind and mute. He healed him, and the man who had been mute could now speak and see.
The crowds were amazed and said, “Could this be the Son of David?”
But the Pharisees, seeing this, said, “This man casts out demons not except by Beelzebul the ruler of demons!”
But knowing their thoughts, he said to them, “Every kingdom divided against itself is destroyed, and every house divided cannot stand.
If Satan casts out Satan, he is divided against himself. How can his kingdom stand?
If I cast out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your own people cast them out? They will be your judges.
But if I cast out demons by the Spirit of God, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.
How can anyone enter a strong man’s house and take his possessions unless he first binds the strong man? Then he can plunder his house.
Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters.
Therefore I tell you, every sin and blasphemy will be forgiven people, but blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven.
Whoever speaks against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven, in this age or in the one to come.
Make the tree good, and its fruit will be good; make the tree bad, and its fruit will be bad, for a tree is known by its fruit.
You brood of vipers! How can you speak good, being evil? For out of the overflow of the heart, the mouth speaks.
A good person brings forth good from the treasure of a good heart, and an evil person brings forth evil from an evil heart.
But I tell you, every careless word people speak will be accounted for on the day of judgment.
For by your words you will be justified, and by your words you will be condemned.”
Then some of the scribes and Pharisees said, “Teacher, we want to see a sign from you.”
He replied, “An evil and adulterous generation seeks a sign, but none will be given except the sign of the prophet Jonah.
For just as Jonah was in the belly of the great fish for three days and three nights, so will the Son of Man be in the heart of the earth for three days and three nights.
The people of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for they repented at the proclamation of Jonah, and behold, something greater than Jonah is here!

The queen of the south will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here!”
When an unclean spirit leaves a person, it wanders through waterless places seeking rest but finds none.
Then it says, ‘I will return to my house from which I came.’ When it returns, it finds the house empty, swept, and put in order.

Then it goes and brings with it seven other spirits more evil than itself, and they settle there. The final state of that person becomes worse than the first. So will it be with this wicked generation also.”
While he was still speaking to the crowds, his mother and brothers stood outside, wanting to speak with him.
Someone told him, “Look, your mother and brothers are standing outside, wanting to speak with you.”
But he replied, “Who is my mother, and who are my brothers?”
Then he stretched out his hand toward his disciples and said, “Here are my mother and my brothers.
For whoever does the will of my Father who is in heaven is my brother, and sister, and mother.”

footnotes.content: The phrase “bread of the Presence” refers to the 12 loaves that were set out continually before YHWH in the tabernacle and temple (see Leviticus 24:5–9). In Hebrew, it is called lechem ha-panim, literally “bread of the face” or “presence,” symbolizing covenant fellowship with God. Only priests were allowed to eat it under normal circumstances. By citing David's exception, Jesus highlights that human need can take precedence over ritual law—a principle grounded in the mercy of God rather than rigid legalism.

In Second-Temple Judaism, the temple was the sacred dwelling place of God—central to worship, forgiveness, and identity. Jesus' statement that “something greater than the temple is here” would have shocked His hearers. He identifies Himself as the true meeting place between God and humanity, anticipating His role as the crucified and risen Son in whom God's presence fully dwells (cf. John 2:19–21; Colossians 2:9).

When Jesus declares Himself “Lord of the Sabbath,” He asserts divine authority over one of the most sacred institutions in Jewish life. The Sabbath was instituted by YHWH at creation (Genesis 2:3) and codified in the Ten Commandments (Exodus 20:8–11). To claim lordship over it is to place Himself above Moses, above the Law, and even above time itself. This is not merely a statement of authority, but a revelation of His divine identity as the One who gave the Sabbath and now fulfills its deepest purpose—rest in God through Christ. The Greek phrase is kyrios tou sabbatou, which mirrors the authority title “Lord” used elsewhere for YHWH, intensifying the claim.

The title “Son of David” was widely recognized as a messianic term rooted in 2 Samuel 7:12–16 and Isaiah 11:1. The crowd's question reflects their amazement and rising hope that Jesus might be the promised King who would restore Israel. However, the phrasing in Greek (mēti houtos estin...?) implies hesitation or doubt—it can be read as “Surely this can't be...?” indicating their internal conflict between astonishment and disbelief.

Jesus refers to Jewish exorcists—perhaps disciples of the Pharisees or other traveling miracle workers—who were recognized as performing exorcisms. His argument exposes the inconsistency of accusing him of demonic collusion while approving their own. By saying “they will be your judges,” Jesus flips the accusation: their approval of other exorcists condemns their rejection of him.
